---
title: "NSF Funding Disruption for Universities"
subtitle: ""
author: "Emily Stern"
institute: "EPPS 6354 · Information Management · UT Dallas · Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, stern.scss]
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: auto
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    footer: "NSF Funding Disruption · Stern · Spring 2026"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Research question

> How has **NSF** grant activity for **U.S. academic institutions** changed since
> the [April 30, 2025 funding freeze]{.anchor-date} and the subsequent
> EO 14332 (Improving Oversight of Federal Grantmaking, Aug 7, 2025),
> particularly across the lifecycle of awards **obligated**, **terminated**, and
> **reinstated**?



## Data sources

| Source | What it provides |
|---|---|
| **USAspending.gov** | Transaction-level NSF assistance data, FY23–FY26 — obligations, deobligations, terminations as recorded |
| **Grants.gov** | NSF opportunity catalog|
| **Grant Watch** | Citizen-science tracker of NSF terminations & reinstatements (Ross + Delaney) |

All three sources are joined into a Postgres schema on Neon and surfaced
through a Shiny dashboard.


## {background-iframe="schema.html" background-interactive="true"}

[](schema.html){.fullscreen-link target="_blank"}

::: {.notes}
Six tables joining Grants.gov, USAspending, and Grant Watch.
Not shown in the diagram: a `snapshots` + `change_log` audit pair —
schema-ready for future Grants.gov diff runs, not populated yet.
:::



## {background-iframe="https://019df1c6-3ad9-2e4e-a581-1a7cb7832261.share.connect.posit.cloud/" background-interactive="true"}

[↗](https://019df1c6-3ad9-2e4e-a581-1a7cb7832261.share.connect.posit.cloud/){.fullscreen-link target="_blank"}



## References & AI use

**Data**

- USAspending.gov — Bulk Assistance extracts, agency code 049, downloaded 2026-04-06
- Grants.gov — NSF opportunity extract, downloaded 2026-04-29
- Grant Watch — Noam Ross (rOpenSci) & Scott Delaney (Harvard)

**AI use disclosure**

This project used generative AI tools (OpenAI GPT models, Anthropic Claude
models) to support writing, literature review, project planning, and database
schema design. Methodological decisions, policy interpretation, source
verification, and final wording were produced and evaluated by the author.
